3: Rice cropping system is the most important anthropogenic sources for methane (CH4), which is a major greenhouse gas (GHG) and has 28 times higher the warming forces than carbon dioxide

(CO2) per unit of weight based on 100-yr global warming potential (GWP) (Pachauri et al., 2014). Total CH4 emissions from rice crop- ping fields is estimated to range within 39-112 Tg yr which covers approximately 5-19% of global CH4 budgets (Menon et al., 2007). To satisfy the demand of an increasing population, global rice production was estimated to increase by 65% from 1990 to 2025 (FAO, 2009), and this expanding rice cultivation may increase global CH4 emissions by around 50% from a 92 Tg CH4 year 1 in 1990 to 131 Tg CH4 year in 2025 (Bouwman, 1991). Soil organic carbon (SOC) is considered as the most key indicator of soil quality and agricultural sustainability, due to its important role on other biological, chemical and physical properties (Delgado

and Follet, 2002). Improvement of soil quality in industrial crop- ping systems could be critical issue to sustain crop productivity and ecosystem sustainability. Livestock manures as organic amendment have been utilized historically to improve soil quality and then increase crop productivity. In other side SOC is regarded as an important sink. The increase of soil C sequestration, which means removal of atmospheric CO2 by storage of stable C as SOM, is accepted as one of powerful options for reducing GHGs in response to climate change (Lal, 2004).

However, the application of organic amendments can tremen- dously increase CH4 emissions under flooded rice cropping systems (Ma et al., 2010; Haque et al., 2013; Kim et al., 2014a & b). The release of labile organic C (OC) compounds from amended organic matter (OM) is an important factor to determine methanogen ac- tivity and CH4 formation rates (Lu et al., 2000; Kim et al., 2014b). Thus, the application of relatively stabilized organic amendments such as biochar and compost could be better options for reducing CH4 emissions in rice cropping fields (Zhang et al., 2010). For example, in Korean paddy fields, we confirmed that composted manure application could decrease seasonal CH4 fluxes during rice cultivation by 20-50%, compared to those in the fresh manure application plots (Kim et al., 2014a; Pramanik and Kim, 2014).

However, because huge amounts of GHGs including CO2, CH4 and nitrous oxide (N20) may be emitted during the composting and transporting processes (Fukumoto et al., 2003; Szanto et al., 2007; Ahn et al., 2011; Zhong et al., 2013), the selection of compost as organic amendment for reducing GHG emission impacts in rice cropping system is still under debate. In the previous study (Jeong et al., 2018), we confirmed via a life cycle assessment (LCA) that compost utilization was more effective than fresh manure to reduce GHG emission impacts in the whole rice cropping chain. The net GWP which was calculated using the sum of GHG emissions from composting and transport processes and during rice cultivation period was approximately 30% lower in compost application than that in fresh manure. However, in this study, the changes of SOC stock via manure application was not considered on evaluating the net GWP on the whole compost utilization process from com- posting to land application in rice paddy condition. To evaluate more precisely the effect of compost utilization on GHG emission impacts in rice cropping systems, the net GWP which included GHG emissions and SOC stock changes should be developed in the whole process.

To evaluate the effect of compost utilization on global warming in the whole rice cropping system, this paper was developed with the following structure. Firstly, we designed the LCA boundary with two main parts (industrial and cropping process) in the whole rice cropping systems (Fig. 1). Secondly, the biogenic GHG fluxes were measured by the closed chamber method during composting and rice cropping processes. Thirdly, SOC stock changes were deter- mined by the NECB analysis in manure amended rice paddies. Finally, the global warming impact was compared using the net GWP, which was computed by summating GHG fluxes and NECB with CO2 equivalence, and the GHGI among the three different treatments (NPK, NPK+fresh manure, and NPK+composted manure application).

8: 2.1. Life cycle assessment (LCA) of greenhouse gas emissions from composting to land application

To evaluate the effect of compost application on suppressing GHG emissions, NPK+composted and NPK+fresh manure treat- ments were installed as the main treatment. NPK plots were additionally included for comparison. The fluxes of GHGs from

composting and rice cropping processes were measured by direct measurement. Impact of GHG emissions during the whole process (from manure management to land application) were analyzed by the LCA method using the Gabi® 4 software (PE International, Stuttgart, Germany).

The model framework was composed with GHG fluxes from two different sources (Fig. 1). The first source was GHG (CH4, N20, and fossil CO2) emissions from industrial processes, which includes diesel supply processes for transportation and machinery opera- tion, and GHG emissions from fertilizer production. These GHG fluxes were based on European Emission Standards 3 and 4 (European Parliament Council, 2000). The second source was three different kinds (CH4, CO2 and N20) of biogenic GHG fluxes from manure composting process and rice cropping system. These GHG fluxes were directly measured by on-site observation.
9: <header>2.2. Selection of local composting model for LCA </header>

To estimate the GHG fluxes from a machinery operation for composting, we selected a medium size of manure composting plant as a representative model in Jinju, Gyeongnam, South Korea. The selected plant produced 5-6 thousand Mg of compost per year using an agitated bay composting system with 6 ha of land. The composting process was composed with active composting (ther- mophilic) stage for three weeks and maturing stage for four weeks. This plant had two agitation lines, and each line had the size of 80 X 10 m in length and width, respectively.

Swine manure was periodically transported using a dump truck (170 kw, 62 kgm) from factory type of pigsties nearby. This fresh manure and the dried manure were mechanically mixed with the ratio of 80:50 (w w-1), respectively, for composting. A front loader (349 kw, 1800 rpm) and a turner (50 kw) were used to build up compost piles and to turn the piles once a day for the active com- posting stage. After the active composting stage, the manure mix- tures were stacked in the open-backyard for post-maturing
10: <header>2.3. Composting, and sampling greenhouse gases during composting </header>

To determine the GHG fluxes from composting piles, the same solid manure was transported from the above composting factory to the experimental station at Gyeongsang National University. Three sets of plastic box having 1.2 m³ in size (width length 1.0 X height 1.2 m) were prepared for composting. This box was covered with 5 cm thick of expanded polystyrene to prevent heat loss. Five hundred sixty-four kg of fresh manure, which had 80.4% (wt wt-1 and 21.9 of the initial moisture content and C/N ratio, respectively (Table 1), was manually mixed with 106 kg of sawdust to adjust the moisture content with around 60% (wt Total 670 kg of manure and sawdust mixture was placed in each plastic box.

The mixture was composted using a conventional static cham- ber method for 50 days in a greenhouse. The active composting phase was considered being completed when the pile temperature stabilized at close to ambient temperature. The piles were manually turned over three times (9, 17 and 30th days after the installation) during composting period. The internal temperature at the middle depth (40-50 of composting pile was daily recorded using a thermometer. The samples were collected using a sampling core (diameter 5 cm X height 5 cm) at three different points, and oven- dried to determine moisture content and bulk density by the gravimetric method. 16: <header>2.5. Determination of greenhouse gas fluxes in rice paddy </header>

Mineralized C loss and GHG fluxes were determined using the closed-chamber method (Rolston, 1986; Kim et al., 2016) in rice field. Two different types of chambers were fixed at the different position. To monitor GHG (CH4 and N20) emission rates, the transparent acrylic chambers having height 112 cm X width 62 cm

xlength 62 cm were installed in each plot which covered 8 hills of rice plant. To determine the heterotrophic respiration (CO2 and CH4) rates, the opaque acrylic column chambers (radius 12 cm and height 20 cm) were installed between rice plants to prevent inter- ference of plant root respiration. Weeds inside of opaque acrylic chamber were properly removed at every sampling time. In two different types of chambers, two holes at the bottom side were installed to permit water flow inside. These holes were closed using rubber stopper only during the time of gas collection. A ther- mometer and a circulating fan were equipped inside each chamber. All of the chambers were kept open except gas sampling times. Once a week, gas was sampled three times (8:00-12:00-16:00 o'clock) a day to make the average daily emission rates. The gas was collected at 0 and 30 min after chamber closing at every sampling.

The concentration of gases was analyzed using gas chromatog- raphy (GC-2010, Shimadzu, Kyoto Japan). The CH4 and CO2 were quantified using a flame ionization detector (FID) equipped with a methanizer. The N20 was quantified using a 63 Ni electron capture detector (ECD). The same column (Porapak NQ column, Q80-100 mesh) and temperature programs were used in two different gas chromatography. The temperature of the methanizer, detector, injector and column were controlled to 350, 110, 100 and 80 °C, respectively. Hydrogen was utilized as carrier gas.

Mineralized C loss and GHG fluxes were determined using the closed-chamber method (Rolston, 1986; Kim et al., 2016) in rice field. Two different types of chambers were fixed at the different position. To monitor GHG (CH4 and N20) emission rates, the transparent acrylic chambers having height 112 cm X width 62 cm

xlength 62 cm were installed in each plot which covered 8 hills of rice plant. To determine the heterotrophic respiration (CO2 and CH4) rates, the opaque acrylic column chambers (radius 12 cm and height 20 cm) were installed between rice plants to prevent inter- ference of plant root respiration. 20: <header>2.6. Calculation of net ecosystem carbon budget (NECB) in rice paddy </header>
In rice paddy fields, SOC stock change was estimated using the NECB analysis (Ma et al., 2013; Hwang et al., 2017):
NECB = C input - C output = (NPP+ Manure + Fertilizer) - (Respired C loss + Harvest removal) (3)

where NPP is the net primary production by rice and weeds. Manure is C input through application of fresh and composted manures. Fertilizer is C inputs by chemical fertilization (urea). Respired C loss is the C outputs through microbial respiration (CO2 and CH4 emission). Harvest removal is the C output through removal of rice straw and grain.
The NPP of rice and weeds during the rice cultivation and fallow seasons was estimated using the following equations, respectively (Smith et al., 2010; Kim et al., 2017):
NPP weeds = NPPaboveground + NPP biomass + NPPlitter + Prhizodeposit

The NPP of rice grain and straw was calculated multiplying the dried biomass productivity and total C content of each part at the harvesting stage. The productivities of other biomass components were calculated using allometric relationships (Gregory, 2006; Ma et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2014). The NPP of root biomass was calcu- lated using the ratio (1.0/0.1) of the aboveground/root (Huang et al., 2007). Litters and rhizodeposits of rice accounted for 5% (Kimura et al., 2004) and 15% of the total biomass (Gregory, 2006; Mandal et al., 2008; Ma et al., 2013), respectively. A grass named Short awn was dominated during the fallow season. To calculate the NPP of grasses, the aboveground and root biomasses were collected in the subsequent mid-May, and the dried biomass productivity and total C content of each part were determined. The NPPs of other components were estimated using the same method mentioned above.

22: <header>2.7. Calculation of GWP and GHGI in the whole process </header>
The overall balance between GHG emission and SOC stock change was calculated in terms of CO2 equivalent (Robertson et al., 2000).
Net GWP (kg CO2-eq. ha-1)=28 x CH4 flux (kg ha-1 + 265 N20 flux (kg ha-1)-NECB(kgCha-1)x 44/12 (6)
The GHGI which means GHG emissions to produce the same crop yield was compared dividing the net GWP by rice grain pro- ductivity (Li et al., 2006; Mosier et al., 2006; Qin et al., 2010).
GHGI (kg CO2-eq. kg-1 grain yield) = Net GWP (kg CO2-eq. ha-1) grain yield (kg ha-1) (7)
2.8. Investigation of yield and chemical properties, and statistical analysis

Rice yield properties were investigated at harvesting stage, and determined following Korean standard methods (RDA, 1995). Rice grain and straw were collected and manually separated. Short awn was dominated as weed during the fallow season. The aboveground and root biomasses of weeds were manually sampled before flood- ing for rice transplanting. The collected biomasses were rinsed using top water, dried at 70 °C for 3 days, and then ground for determining total C content. Total C and N contents of plant biomasses and ma- nures were quantified using an automated true macro element analyzer (CHNS-932 Analyzer; Leco, Saint joseph, USA).

SAS software (version 9.3, SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC) was used for statistical analyses. A one-way ANOVA was conducted to compare the means of the different treatments. Tukey's honest significant difference (HSD) test was used to determine treatment effects.

24: To evaluate the impact of compost utilization on global warming in the whole rice cropping system, three major GHG (CH4, CO2 and N20) fluxes were firstly determined during composting and rice cropping processes. Secondly, soil C stock changes via manure ap- plications were estimated using NECB analysis in rice paddy. Herein, as a main C input source, the NPP of rice plant and weeds was measured at the end of rice cropping and fallow season. As a C
output source, the mineralized C loss (heterotrophic CO2 emission) was monitored during a year. Finally, the impact of manure utili- zation on global warming was compared using net GWP and GHGI.
3.1. Greenhouse gas fluxes during manure composting process

To evaluate the maturity of manure compost, the important chemical and physical properties (temperature, moisture content, pH, electrical conductivity, and total C and N contents) of compost piles were periodically monitored for 50 days of composting (Supplementary material-1). The temperature inside the compost pile inclined speedily up to 60 °C right after manure piling. This high temperature was maintained for around 15 days, and then gradually declined to background temperature. After 30 days of composting, the pile temperature was not any more increased by turning and moisturizing. Moisture content was gradually

decreased with composting, but maintained within 52-61% (wt vwt-1 during the whole composting process. The pH of manure piles was slightly decreased from 9.0 to 8.8, and then stabilized. The EC of compost piles was 6.4 dS m -Superscript(1) at the initial stage, highly increased with composting, and then stabilized at 7.0 ds m after 13 days of composting.

The typical emission patterns of biogenic GHGs (CO2, CH4 and N20) were observed during composting period (Fig. 2). Carbon dioxide and N20 emission rates were similarly fluctuated with the temperature changes of manure pile during composting process. In comparison, the high rate of CH4 emission was observed at the initial stage of composting, but thereafter its emission rate was sharply dropped to the background level and stabilized. Total fluxes of CO2, CH4 and N20 for 50 days of composting were estimated 1333, 0.41 and 0.16 kg Mg 1 compost on a dry weight basis, respectively (Fig. 2).

Fig. 2. Changes of biogenic greenhouse gas emissions during cattle manure composting. Different letters indicate honestly significantly different at 5% level. Bars represent standard deviations (n = 3).

28: <header>3.2. Greenhouse gas emissions in rice paddy field </header>

To estimate the impact of manure applications on GHG fluxes, two different GHG (CH4 and N20) emission rates were periodically monitored for a year (Fig. 3). Typical emission patterns of two GHGs were observed during the investigation period. Irrespective with soil amendments, high rates of CH4 were emitted during the irri- gated rice cultivation season, but very low rates of CH4 consump- tion and emission were observed during the cold and dried fallow season.

During rice cropping season, CH4 flux was relatively low at the initial growing stage, and then considerably increased with plant growth and the development of reductive soil conditions. After rice flowering stage, CH4 emission was gradually decreased to the background level. However, manure application significantly increased total CH4 fluxes by 42-81% over 351 kg ha 1 of CH4 flux in NPK application plot (control). Comparatively, total CH4 flux was approximately 20% lower in the NPK+composted manure plots than total CH4 flux (634 kg ha in the NPK+fresh manure treatment.

Different with CH4 emissions, very low levels of N20 were observed during the whole investigation period (Fig. 3). The two different types of manure applications largely increased N20 emissions with similar patterns. The annual N20 flux was 14.3 kg ha in NPK application, but manure applications increased N20 fluxes by 10-37%. This increase was slightly higher in the NPK+fresh manure plot than 15.7 kg ha- in the NPK+composted manure.

30: <header>3.3. Rice productivity and net primary production (NPP) </header>

Total 2 Mg ha-Superscript(1) of composted and fresh manures on dried weight basis was applied in two different manure treatments before rice transplanting. Manure application significantly increased rice grain yields by 12-17% over 5.7 Mg ha of NPK application, but grain productivities were not significantly different between fresh and composted manure treatments (Table 2). In particular, manure application reacted more effectively to straw and total biomass yields than grain yield. Two different manure applications increased straw and total biomass yields by 15-20 and 14-18% than NPK treatment, respectively.

Total C concentration of each part (grain, straw and root) of rice biomass was not different among the treatments (data not shown). Therefore, the NPP of rice plant was decided by total biomass productivity (Table 2). Irrespective with soil amendments, the NPP of grain and straw covered approximately 75-80% of total NPP, and then followed by rhizodeposit (11%), root and litter. Manure application significantly increased the NPP of rice plant by 14-19% over NPK application, but there was no meaningful difference be- tween fresh and composted manure treatments.

36: <header>3.5. Net ecosystem carbon budget (NECB) in rice cropping system </header>

To determine the effect of manure applications on soil C stock changes, the NECB was calculated using the difference between C input and output in rice cropping system (Table 3). Total C input which was composed with the NPP of rice and weeds, fertilizer (Urea-C) and manure was approximately 7.7 Mg C ha in the NPK treatment. However, the NPP of rice and weeds covered more than 99% of total C input. Manure application significantly increased the input by 26-32% over the NPK treatment, mainly due to increased rice biomass productivity. However, there was no big difference between fresh and composted manures.

Total C output which was composed with harvest C removal and respired C loss (CO2 and CH4) was approximately 8.2 Mg C ha in the NPK treatment. In the selected region rice straw is generally removed for livestock feeding, and therefore the harvest C removal was the main C output source in the field condition. NPK+com- posted manure application increased total C output only 13% over the NPK treatment, mainly due to harvest removal, but NPK+fresh manure increased total C output by around 40%, due to the addi- tional increase of respired C loss. NPK+composted manure appli- cation increased only 11% of the respired C loss over the NPK treatment, but NPK+fresh manure increased this C loss by approximately 90%. In particular, NPK+fresh manure application largely increased methanized C loss which covered 17% of respired C loss, comparable with around 3-8% covering in the NPK and NPK+composted manure treatments.

40: <header>3.6. Net global warming potential and greenhouse gas intensity in whole rice cultivation system </header>

To evaluate the whole impact of GHG emissions from com- posting process to compost application, the net GWP which was summated by GHG fluxes and SOC stock change (NECB) with CO2 equivalent was compared among the treatments (Table 4). In the NPK treatment, the net GWP was approximately 15.5 Mg CO2-eq. ha-1 The GHG emissions during rice cropping decided this net GWP size, but the GHG emission during industrial process negli- gibly contributed. Methane covered about 60% of the net GWP, and followed by N20 emission (24%) and SOC depletion (3%).

Manure applications significantly increased the net GWP. Composted manure increased the net GWP by approximately 26% over the NPK treatment, due to additional GHG emissions during composting process. Manure composting additionally increased the net GWP by 2.8 Mg CO2-eq. ha which covered approximately 14% of total GHG emissions (19.5 Mg CO2-eq. ha for the whole process. However, NPK+fresh manure application increased the net GWP by more than 80% over the NPK treatment, due to highly increased GHG emissions, in particular CH4, during rice cultivation.

Manure application did not only increased rice grain yields by 12-17% over the NPK treatment (Fig. 5), but also significantly increased GHG emissions (Table 4). In the other side, the SOC constitutes a large pool of C in the global C cycle, representing a dynamic balance between C input and output (Stewart et al., 2007). Carbon is added into soil ecosystem through photosynthesis and deposition, and C is also removed via respira- tion, leaching and erosion losses. The SOC accumulation can convert atmospheric CO2 into stable OC pools in the soil, which can mitigate global warming (Paustian et al., 2004). Thus, increasing the SOC stock can be an important issue for achieving desirable soil functions and sequestering atmospheric CO2.

Livestock manures as an organic amendment have been widely utilized to improve soil quality and crop productivity. We also confirmed that NPK plus 2 Mg ha of fresh and composted manure applications significantly increased rice productivity by approxi- mately 10-20% over NPK application (Fig. 5). In Korea, only 90 and 19.6 kg ha- of N and P are recommended for rice cultivation (RDA, 2017). However, these levels were developed mainly for environ- ment protection, not for maximum yield. In this study, 2 Mg ha-Superscript(1) of fresh and composted manure application could additionally supply

37-42 and 14-161 kg ha of N and P, respectively, and then significantly increased rice productivity. Incorporation of manure positively influences soil nutrient-carrying capacity (Manna et al., 2005) and bacterial communities and promote rice growth by supplying essential nutrients to rice plant (Yan et al., 2007; Das et al., 2017). Manure applications significantly increased tillering number of rice hill (Supplementary material-2). However, rice grain yields were not significantly different between fresh and com- posted manures (Fig. 5).

Manure application for rice cultivation significantly increased total C input, mainly due to the increasing NPP of rice biomass (Table 3). Two Mg ha of different manure applications increased the NPP of rice plant by approximately 14% over NPK application. However, total C input was not different between fresh and com- posted manures. In comparison, the C added via manure applica- tion and the NPP of weeds grown during the fallow season did not change meaningfully the total C input scale. More than 85% of total C input was contributed by the NPP of rice plant, and then followed by manure C (8% of total C input) and the NPP of weeds (5-6% of total C input) (Table 3).

Total C output was composed with harvest C removal and respired C loss in rice paddy (Hwang et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2017). Manure application significantly increased harvest C removal and respired C loss (Table 3). In the selected region most of rice straw is removed for cattle feeding. Harvested C removal covered approxi- mately 58-70% of total C output, but manure applications signifi- cantly increase harvest C removal, due to yield increase. Manures applications significantly increased respired C loss (Table 3), which may be attributed to increased availability of labile OC from ma- nures application (Inubushi et al., 2003; Sasada et al., 2011). Rice is cultivated under the flooded soil conditions for more than 100 days, and then SOC can be mineralized to CO2 and CH4 (Janzen, 2004; Mosier et al., 2005). However, most of mineralized C was respired into CO2, but CH4 contribution was negligible (Mosier et al., 2006; Ma et al., 2013). Carbon dioxide covered 97% of the respired C loss in NPK treatment, but this coverage decreased to 83-92% in the manure applied plots. NPK+fresh manure application increased respired C loss by approximately 90% than NPK application, but NPK+composted manure by only 11%. The easily decomposable OC in fresh manure significantly increased respired C loss by stimu- lating the activity of soil microorganisms (De Nobili et al., 2001), whereas more stabilized C addition via composting was effective to decrease respired C loss (Kim et al., 2014a). Interestingly, NPK+fresh manure application largely increased the ratio of CH4-C to CO2-C by 0.2, comparing with 0.09 in the NPK+composted manure plot (Table 3).

Under the conventional agricultural practices, in which only chemical fertilizer was added and rice straw was removed at har- vesting stage, the NECB which means the difference between C input and output (Chapin et al., 2006; Smith et al., 2010) showed minus value (-419 kg ha-1 yr-1) (Table 3) (Hwang et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2017). This indicates this level of SOC might be depleted annually under the present soil management. However, NPK plus 2 Mg of composted manure application converted the NECB with small plus value (503 kg C ha yr-1), which means SOC might not be depleted even under straw removal. Different with compost application, same level of fresh manure application largely decreased the NECB to minus 1.3 Mg C ha yr-1, due to highly increased respired C loss. NPK plus 2 Mg ha of fresh manure application added only 0.78 Mg C ha of manure C, but increased the respired C loss by approximately 4.8 Mg C ha which was not comparable with 2.8 Mg C ha-¹ in the NPK+composted manure plots. It might be caused by the priming effect of labile C and N addition via fresh manure application (Fontaine et al., 2003). In many field observations (Löhnis, 1926; Broadbent, 1948; Broadbent

and Bartholomew, 1949; Bingeman et al., 1953; Broadbent and Nakashima, 1974; Sorensen, 1974; Wu et al., 1993), the addition of fresh OM (i.e., green manure, straw etc.) in a soil significantly intensified SOC mineralization and then gradually depleted SOC stock.

Under the conventional soil management (control) the net GWP was approximately 15.5 CO2-eq. ha in the whole rice cropping system from the industrial process (fertilizer manufacturing, transport, etc.) to rice cropping (Table 4). Most of GHGs were emitted in rice paddy field, and only less than 3% of GHGs were emitted from the industrial process (Jeong et al., 2018). The CH4 flux covered approximately 60% of the net GWP, and then followed by N20 flux (24%) and SOC depletion (3%). In comparison, the contri- bution of the industrial process on GHG emissions was negligible. NPK plus 2 Mg ha of fresh manure application significantly increased the net GWP by more than 80% compared to the NPK application, mainly due to highly increased CH4 emissions during rice cultivation. NPK+fresh manure application increased total CH4 fluxes by more than 80% over the NPK treatment. The CH4 fluxes covered approximately 60% of the net GWP during the whole process, and then followed by the N20 emissions (18%) and SOC depletion (4.3%). Manure composting added total 2.6 Mg CO2-eq. of GHG emissions and the composted manure application increased the net GWP by approximately 8% than the NPK application in rice paddy. As a result, manure composting and compost application increased the net GWP by approximately 26% than the NPK treat- ment. However, it was around 30% lower than 28 Mg CO2-eq. ha in the NPK+fresh manure application. Same level of manure was applied in two different manure treatments, but NPK+compost application significantly decreased CH4 emissions and increased SOC stock, comparing with the NPK+fresh manure application. Two different types of manure application significantly increased rice grain yield and the net GWP during the whole pro- cess. Comparing with GHGI (2.7 CO2-eq. kg-1 grain), which divided the net GWP by grain productivity (Li et al., 2006; Mosier et al., 2006; Qin et al., 2010), in the NPK application, NPK+com- posted manure application did not increase GHGI (Fig. 5), mainly due to increased grain productivity. However, NPK+fresh manure application significantly increased GHGI by 54% than the NPK treatment. This means aerobic composting added new GHG emis- sions, but this increase is not comparable with the effect of compost on decreasing GHG emissions in rice paddy, in particular CH4 emissions.

Carbon is added into soil ecosystem through photosynthesis and deposition, and C is also removed via respira- tion, leaching and erosion losses. The SOC accumulation can convert atmospheric CO2 into stable OC pools in the soil, which can mitigate global warming (Paustian et al., 2004). Thus, increasing the SOC stock can be an important issue for achieving desirable soil functions and sequestering atmospheric CO2.

Livestock manures as an organic amendment have been widely utilized to improve soil quality and crop productivity. We also confirmed that NPK plus 2 Mg ha of fresh and composted manure applications significantly increased rice productivity by approxi- mately 10-20% over NPK application (Fig. 5). In Korea, only 90 and 19.6 kg ha- of N and P are recommended for rice cultivation (RDA, 2017). However, these levels were developed mainly for environ- ment protection, not for maximum yield. In this study, 2 Mg ha-Superscript(1) of fresh and composted manure application could additionally supply

37-42 and 14-161 kg ha of N and P, respectively, and then significantly increased rice productivity. Incorporation of manure positively influences soil nutrient-carrying capacity (Manna et al., 2005) and bacterial communities and promote rice growth by supplying essential nutrients to rice plant (Yan et al., 2007; Das et al., 2017). Manure applications significantly increased tillering number of rice hill (Supplementary material-2). However, rice grain yields were not significantly different between fresh and com- posted manures (Fig. 5).

Manure application for rice cultivation significantly increased total C input, mainly due to the increasing NPP of rice biomass (Table 3). Two Mg ha of different manure applications increased the NPP of rice plant by approximately 14% over NPK application. However, total C input was not different between fresh and com- posted manures. In comparison, the C added via manure applica- tion and the NPP of weeds grown during the fallow season did not change meaningfully the total C input scale. More than 85% of total C input was contributed by the NPP of rice plant, and then followed by manure C (8% of total C input) and the NPP of weeds (5-6% of total C input) (Table 3).

Total C output was composed with harvest C removal and respired C loss in rice paddy (Hwang et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2017). Manure application significantly increased harvest C removal and respired C loss (Table 3). In the selected region most of rice straw is removed for cattle feeding. Harvested C removal covered approxi- mately 58-70% of total C output, but manure applications signifi- cantly increase harvest C removal, due to yield increase. Manures applications significantly increased respired C loss (Table 3), which may be attributed to increased availability of labile OC from ma- nures application (Inubushi et al., 2003; Sasada et al., 2011). Rice is cultivated under the flooded soil conditions for more than 100 days, and then SOC can be mineralized to CO2 and CH4 (Janzen, 2004; Mosier et al., 2005). However, most of mineralized C was respired into CO2, but CH4 contribution was negligible (Mosier et al., 2006; Ma et al., 2013). Carbon dioxide covered 97% of the respired C loss in NPK treatment, but this coverage decreased to 83-92% in the manure applied plots. NPK+fresh manure application increased respired C loss by approximately 90% than NPK application, but NPK+composted manure by only 11%. The easily decomposable OC in fresh manure significantly increased respired C loss by stimu- lating the activity of soil microorganisms (De Nobili et al., 2001), whereas more stabilized C addition via composting was effective to decrease respired C loss (Kim et al., 2014a). Interestingly, NPK+fresh manure application largely increased the ratio of CH4-C to CO2-C by 0.2, comparing with 0.09 in the NPK+composted manure plot (Table 3).

Under the conventional agricultural practices, in which only chemical fertilizer was added and rice straw was removed at har- vesting stage, the NECB which means the difference between C input and output (Chapin et al., 2006; Smith et al., 2010) showed minus value (-419 kg ha-1 yr-1) (Table 3) (Hwang et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2017). This indicates this level of SOC might be depleted annually under the present soil management. However, NPK plus 2 Mg of composted manure application converted the NECB with small plus value (503 kg C ha yr-1), which means SOC might not be depleted even under straw removal. Different with compost application, same level of fresh manure application largely decreased the NECB to minus 1.3 Mg C ha yr-1, due to highly increased respired C loss. NPK plus 2 Mg ha of fresh manure application added only 0.78 Mg C ha of manure C, but increased the respired C loss by approximately 4.8 Mg C ha which was not comparable with 2.8 Mg C ha-¹ in the NPK+composted manure plots. It might be caused by the priming effect of labile C and N addition via fresh manure application (Fontaine et al., 2003). In many field observations (Löhnis, 1926; Broadbent, 1948; Broadbent

and Bartholomew, 1949; Bingeman et al., 1953; Broadbent and Nakashima, 1974; Sorensen, 1974; Wu et al., 1993), the addition of fresh OM (i.e., green manure, straw etc.) in a soil significantly intensified SOC mineralization and then gradually depleted SOC stock.

Under the conventional soil management (control) the net GWP was approximately 15.5 CO2-eq. ha in the whole rice cropping system from the industrial process (fertilizer manufacturing, transport, etc.) to rice cropping (Table 4). Most of GHGs were emitted in rice paddy field, and only less than 3% of GHGs were emitted from the industrial process (Jeong et al., 2018). The CH4 flux covered approximately 60% of the net GWP, and then followed by N20 flux (24%) and SOC depletion (3%). In comparison, the contri- bution of the industrial process on GHG emissions was negligible. NPK plus 2 Mg ha of fresh manure application significantly increased the net GWP by more than 80% compared to the NPK application, mainly due to highly increased CH4 emissions during rice cultivation. NPK+fresh manure application increased total CH4 fluxes by more than 80% over the NPK treatment. The CH4 fluxes covered approximately 60% of the net GWP during the whole process, and then followed by the N20 emissions (18%) and SOC depletion (4.3%). Manure composting added total 2.6 Mg CO2-eq. of GHG emissions and the composted manure application increased the net GWP by approximately 8% than the NPK application in rice paddy. As a result, manure composting and compost application increased the net GWP by approximately 26% than the NPK treat- ment. However, it was around 30% lower than 28 Mg CO2-eq. ha in the NPK+fresh manure application. Same level of manure was applied in two different manure treatments, but NPK+compost application significantly decreased CH4 emissions and increased SOC stock, comparing with the NPK+fresh manure application. Two different types of manure application significantly increased rice grain yield and the net GWP during the whole pro- cess. Comparing with GHGI (2.7 CO2-eq. kg-1 grain), which divided the net GWP by grain productivity (Li et al., 2006; Mosier et al., 2006; Qin et al., 2010), in the NPK application, NPK+com- posted manure application did not increase GHGI (Fig. 5), mainly due to increased grain productivity. However, NPK+fresh manure application significantly increased GHGI by 54% than the NPK treatment. This means aerobic composting added new GHG emis- sions, but this increase is not comparable with the effect of compost on decreasing GHG emissions in rice paddy, in particular CH4 emissions.

51: Fresh and composted manure application on dry weight basis) significantly increased rice yields by 12-17% and the NPP as a C input source by 14-19% over the NPK treatment. How- ever, rice yield and NPP were not different between two manures. NPK+fresh manure application significantly increased the net GWP by 80% over the NPK treatment, mainly due to increased CH4 emissions and decreased SOC stock in rice cropping systems. In comparison, approximately 2.8 Mg CO2-eq. of GHGs was addition- ally emitted in 2 Mg of manure compost making process, but these GHG emissions from industrial processes did not significantly in- fluence the net GWP in the whole rice cropping system, due to significantly reduced CH4 emissions and increased SOC stock. The GHGI was similar between the NPK and NPK+composted manure treatments, but approximately 50% higher in the NPK+fresh manure treatment than the NPK+composted manure. Therefore, composting and compost application could be a good management strategy to improve soil productivity and minimize the GHG emission impacts in rice paddy ecosystems.
